mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Chile on?
mapQuestions.answer: South America. Chile is the long, narrow country on the western coast, with the Pacific Ocean on one side and the Andes Mountains on the other 🌎

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Chile?
mapQuestions.answer: Santiago, in a valley between the coastal mountains and the Andes. About 7 million people live in the metro area.

mapQuestions.question: How long is Chile from north to south?
mapQuestions.answer: About 4,300 km — the longest country in the world. But it averages only 175 km wide. If you laid it on Europe, it would stretch from Norway to Algeria.

mapQuestions.question: What is the driest desert in the world?
mapQuestions.answer: The Atacama, in northern Chile. Some weather stations there have NEVER recorded rain. NASA tests Mars rovers in the Atacama because it is the closest place on Earth to Mars.

mapQuestions.question: Where is Easter Island?
mapQuestions.answer: Around 3,700 km off the Chilean coast in the middle of the Pacific Ocean. Politically Chilean, but culturally Polynesian, home to the 887 giant Moai stone heads.

foods.name: Empanada de pino
foods.description: A baked pastry filled with beef, onion, a hard-boiled egg, an olive and a raisin. Found at every market and Sunday lunch table.

foods.name: Completo
foods.description: A Chilean hot dog: a sausage in a bun, topped with crushed avocado, mayonnaise, chopped tomato, and sometimes sauerkraut. Looks ridiculous, tastes brilliant.

foods.name: Pastel de choclo
foods.description: A baked corn pie. Sweet corn paste covers a layer of seasoned beef, chicken, hard-boiled egg, raisins and olives, baked in a clay pot until golden on top.

foods.name: Sopaipilla
foods.description: A fried pumpkin-dough flatbread, salty or sweet. Soaked in spiced syrup (sopaipillas pasadas) in winter; eaten plain or with mustard the rest of the year.

foods.name: Cazuela
foods.description: A clear, hearty country soup with a whole chunk of meat, a piece of corn cob, a potato, a slice of pumpkin and rice. Pure Chilean comfort food.

foods.name: Pacific seafood
foods.description: Chile has 4,000 km of coastline, with some of the freshest seafood on Earth. Sea urchin, mussels, razor clams, conger eel and ceviche are all classics.

scavengerItems.label: The Andes mountains visible from the city
A guanaco or vicuña (wild llama relatives) on a hillside
A red Atacama desert landscape or salt flat
A vineyard, wine sign, or bottle of Chilean wine
A penguin colony on the southern coast
A friendly street dog (Chile is full of well-looked-after strays)
A street mural in Valparaíso's painted hills
A real empanada de pino, hot from the oven
A Moai (Easter Island stone head) replica or image
A sign for a copper mine (Chile is the world's top copper producer)
A clear desert night with thousands of visible stars

geoMatches.description: The capital, in a valley ringed by snow-capped mountains. Modern skyscrapers, sprawling markets, old wooden houses, and the highest mountain view of any capital in the Americas.
The driest non-polar desert in the world. Some places have never recorded rain. After a rare downpour (every 5–7 years), millions of wildflowers bloom — the "Desierto Florido".
3,700 km off the Chilean coast in the middle of the Pacific. 887 enormous Moai stone heads carved by the Rapa Nui people centuries ago. One of the most remote inhabited places on Earth.
A national park in Chilean Patagonia. Three granite spires rise out of the ice. Wind, glaciers, lakes, guanacos. Most beautiful place in the country, depending on who you ask.
A port city on the Pacific coast. Steep hills covered in brightly painted wooden houses, old funicular elevators, and some of the best street art in the world.
Southern Chile, around Puerto Varas and Pucón. German-style towns built by 19th-century settlers, ringed by volcanoes and deep blue lakes.

senses.smell: Pacific sea air, dust on the Atacama wind, eucalyptus on the breeze, hot sopaipillas frying in oil, copper smelters in the north, the bitter scent of pisco distilling…
senses.hear: Chilean Spanish (often called the fastest in the world), "cachái?" tagged at the end of sentences, "po" filler words, Andean pan flutes, surf on rocky beaches, distant volcanic rumbles…
senses.taste: Salty Pacific shellfish, sweet pisco sour, hot empanada with juicy filling, deep Chilean Cabernet, mote con huesillo (peach and wheat-grain drink), citrusy ceviche…
senses.feel: Dry desert wind, cold Pacific waves, the breath-tightening burn of Andean altitude, soft alpaca wool, hot empanada burning your fingers, sticky humid Patagonian rain…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How can one country be so long and so thin?
stories.body: Chile is 4,300 km long from north to south but only 175 km wide on average. If you laid it on Europe, it would stretch from Norway to Algeria. The reason is the Andes mountains — Chile is basically a long sloping strip of land between the world's longest mountain range and the Pacific Ocean. The country contains one of the driest deserts on Earth (Atacama), one of the wettest fjord regions on Earth (Patagonia), 4,000 km of Pacific coast, 90 active volcanoes, the longest mountain range in the world, and even a claim to part of Antarctica. Driving from one end to the other takes weeks.

stories.question: Why does NASA test Mars rovers in Chile?
stories.body: The Atacama Desert in northern Chile is the driest non-polar desert in the world. Some weather stations have NEVER recorded rainfall. NASA uses it to test Mars rovers because it is the closest place on Earth to actual Mars: dry red soil, intense UV, almost no plants, no insects, no clouds. The Atacama is also home to the world's biggest astronomical observatories (ALMA, La Silla, Paranal, the giant new ELT) because the dry, thin, clear air gives the best view of space anywhere on Earth. Chile is the country where humanity does most of its looking out at the universe.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If you could walk on Mars or stand in the Atacama and look up at the same stars — which would you pick?

stories.question: Who carved the Moai?
stories.body: In the middle of the Pacific, 3,700 km from anywhere, sits Easter Island (called Rapa Nui in the local language). Polynesian sailors reached this tiny island hundreds of years ago. They carved 887 enormous stone heads called Moai, some 10 metres tall and weighing up to 80 tonnes, then somehow moved them across the island and stood them up on platforms facing inland. Nobody knew for certain how they moved them, until a modern experiment in 2012 proved you could "walk" a Moai upright using ropes, three at a time, just like the Rapa Nui legends always said. The island became Chilean in 1888.

stories.question: Who was Salvador Allende, and who was Pinochet?
stories.body: In 1970 Chile elected Salvador Allende, the first ever Marxist president of a country anywhere in the world. He started nationalising businesses, including American-owned copper mines. On 11 September 1973 the army staged a coup. Allende died inside the presidential palace. The new dictator, General Augusto Pinochet, ruled for 17 years. Thousands of his opponents were tortured, killed or "disappeared". Many fled Chile and were taken in as refugees by countries like the UK, Sweden and Mexico. Pinochet was finally voted out in a referendum in 1988. He was arrested in London in 1998 but never tried in court. Chile is still working through what happened in those 17 years.

stories.question: Why is copper such a big deal in Chile?
stories.body: Chile produces around 28% of all the copper in the world. Copper is in every phone, every wire, every electric motor, every solar panel, and every electric car. As the world moves to renewable energy, demand for copper is rising fast. Chile's biggest copper mine, Chuquicamata, is the largest open-pit copper mine on Earth — a hole 4.3 km long, 3 km wide, and 850 metres deep. You can see it from space. Copper income built modern Chile, but it has also caused some of its biggest problems — pollution, indigenous land disputes, and big political fights over who should benefit from the country's natural wealth.
stories.thinkingQuestion: When a country gets rich from one resource (oil, copper, gold), what should it do with the money?

convoQuestions.question: Best Chilean food you have eaten so far?
Mountains, desert, ocean, city, or end-of-the-world Patagonia — which has been your favourite?
Chilean Spanish is famously fast. Can you understand more or less than other Spanish accents?
Have you had a pisco sour or a Chilean wine, watched the sunset, and felt very smug about it?
Chile is so long it has six different climate zones. What's the most extreme one you've been in?
The Atacama is the best place on Earth to look at the stars. If you could move there for a year, would you?

wordSearch.hint: Chilean foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Quiltro (Chilean street dog)
animals.description: Friendly, mostly cared for by neighbourhoods, often follow you for blocks.

animals.name: Guanaco
animals.description: Wild llama-relative, in Patagonia (Torres del Paine) and the Atacama altiplano.

animals.name: Andean condor
animals.description: 3-metre wingspan, soaring above the Andean ridges. National bird.

animals.name: James's flamingo
animals.description: On high-altitude salt lakes in the Atacama. Pink against turquoise water.

animals.name: Magellanic penguin
animals.description: Off the Patagonian coasts and on Isla Magdalena.

animals.name: Puma
animals.description: Torres del Paine has the best wild puma sightings in the world.
